title: How Britain Became as Poor as Mississippi - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Alex Tabarrok highlights an Atlantic piece by Idrees Kahloon cataloguing Britain's economic decline — from the NHS spending more on maternity-malpractice claims than on maternity care, to a tripled-cost, still-unfinished HS2 rail line and a £700 million 'fish disco' at Hinkley Point C — and argues the U.S. is starting to resemble a 'declining Great Britain' propped up by a few dynamic sectors like AI, with neither immigration nor trade explaining the shared inability to build infrastructure.

insights.claim: Britain's National Health Service now spends more money settling maternity-malpractice claims than it spends on actually providing maternity care.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A stark fiscal indicator of state-capacity dysfunction in the UK's flagship public health system, relevant to assessing British public-sector cost trajectories and litigation-driven fiscal drag.
insights.quote: The health service now has to spend more money settling maternity-malpractice claims than it does on actually providing maternity care.

insights.claim: In a 2023 survey, one in ten Britons reported doing DIY dental work, in extreme cases extracting their own teeth or gluing broken crowns back together, due to inability to obtain NHS or affordable private dental care.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A concrete healthcare-access breakdown statistic that signals real erosion of UK public-service delivery, relevant for pricing UK sovereign/public-sector risk narratives.
insights.quote: one in 10 reported doing DIY dental work, in extreme cases extracting their own teeth or gluing broken crowns back together.

insights.claim: British junior doctors struck for the 15th time in three years over pay that starts at just £38,800, while the median salary for British civil servants is £35,680.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Quantifies UK public-sector wage stagnation and labor unrest, a leading indicator of continued strike risk and public-service strain in Britain.
insights.quote: Junior doctors recently went on strike for the 15th time in three years over their salaries, which start at just £38,800; the median salary for British civil servants is £35,680.

insights.claim: Prime Minister Keir Starmer drew tabloid criticism from the Daily Mail for vacationing at a Valencia hotel costing £200 a night, illustrating how modest elite British lifestyles now look relative to American standards.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: An anecdotal but telling marker of how far British living standards, even at the top, have fallen relative to American comparables — useful color for UK-vs-US standard-of-living comparisons.
insights.quote: vacationing in Valencia, Spain, at what the tabloid described as a luxury hotel, costing £200 a night.
insights.locator: In April, amid the Iran conflict

insights.claim: Since HS2 was proposed in 2009, its cost has tripled to more than £100 billion, making it the world's most expensive rail line even as the most important sections connecting to Manchester and Leeds have been cut, with completion of the truncated Birmingham-to-outer-London route not expected until 2040.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A benchmark case of infrastructure cost blowout and scope collapse, directly relevant to comparing UK, California, and other Western megaproject execution risk.
insights.quote: its costs have tripled, to more than £100 billion. It is the most expensive rail line in the world.
insights.locator: Recent plans to transform the country

insights.claim: A special structure to protect a rare bat species along the HS2 route in Buckinghamshire required 8,000 permits and cost £216 million to build.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A vivid, citable example of regulatory/permitting overreach driving infrastructure costs, useful shorthand evidence in permitting-reform arguments.
insights.quote: A special structure to protect a rare bat species near the rail line in Buckinghamshire required 8,000 permits and was built at a cost of £216 million.

insights.claim: Britain's Hinkley Point C is the world's most expensive nuclear power plant despite still being unbuilt; its environmental-impact assessment ran 31,401 pages and includes a £700 million 'fish disco' underwater sound system to deter fish from the plant's intake pipes.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Another headline data point on regulatory-driven cost inflation in UK energy infrastructure, directly relevant to assessing UK nuclear buildout economics and permitting-reform urgency.
insights.quote: its environmental-impact assessment ran 31,401 pages; the plant will feature a £700 million “fish disco,” which will pulse sounds underwater to deter animals from its intake pipes.
insights.locator: Britain also hosts the world’s most expensive

insights.claim: Tabarrok argues the United States increasingly resembles a 'declining Great Britain' propped up by only one or two dynamic sectors, most notably AI, rather than being categorically different from Britain's trajectory.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A direct thesis that U.S. growth is narrowly dependent on the AI sector to offset broader stagnation risk — relevant to how concentrated U.S. equity leadership in AI should be read against underlying structural decline elsewhere in the economy.
insights.quote: the United States looks a lot less like a shining city on a hill and a lot more like a declining Great Britain, appendaged with one or two dynamic sectors, most notably AI.
insights.locator: Americans are likely to come away

insights.claim: Tabarrok argues that neither immigration restriction nor anti-trade policy (Brexit functioning as the equivalent of a high tariff regime) explains Britain's, or California's, inability to build infrastructure like high-speed rail.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Rejects nativist and protectionist narratives as false explanations for infrastructure stagnation, pointing instead toward permitting and regulatory process as the real bottleneck — relevant framing for evaluating both UK and US 'abundance agenda' policy debates.
insights.quote: Needless to say, neither immigrants nor trade explain Britain’s—or California’s—inability to build high-speed rail or other infrastructure.

insights.claim: Tabarrok criticizes the American right for fixating on symbolic culture-war fights like birthright citizenship and the American left for nominating candidates with 'Marxist-Leninist sympathies,' arguing both sides avoid the real problem of an inability to build, and singles out the abundance and progress movements as genuine solutions.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Positions the 'abundance agenda' (deregulation/permitting reform) as the credible political solution set to watch, versus culture-war politics on both sides that Tabarrok views as a distraction from the real economic problem.
insights.quote: the right gets literally hysterical over symbolic culture-war questions like birthright citizenship, while the left nominates candidates with Marxist-Leninist sympathies.
